touchableopacity.subtitle: A pressable wrapper that dims its content during a touch by animating opacity.
touchableopacity.description:
`TouchableOpacity` is a pressable wrapper that dims its content when touched by animating the opacity of an internal `Animated.View`. It is one of the original touch primitives in React Native and still widely used, but the React Native team recommends `Pressable` for new code.

The opacity during a press is controlled by `activeOpacity`, which defaults to `0.2` — a very strong dim. Most designs set it to `0.7` or `0.8` for subtler feedback. Remember that a lower value means more transparent, not less.

Because the animation runs on an `Animated.View` wrapping your children, it adds a layer to the view hierarchy. This can occasionally cause unexpected layout behaviour — particularly with absolutely positioned children or overlapping views.

props.activeOpacity: Opacity of the wrapper during a press; defaults to `0.2` — lower values produce a stronger dimming effect.
props.onLongPress: Called when a press is held longer than 500ms.

gotchas.gotcha1: *Prefer Pressable for new code* — TouchableOpacity is the older API; the React Native team recommends `Pressable` for new development as it offers more control over the press lifecycle.
gotchas.gotcha2: *activeOpacity defaults to 0.2* — This is very dim; most designs use `0.7`–`0.8` for subtler feedback. Remember: lower value = more transparent.
gotchas.gotcha3: *Animated.View wrapper affects layout* — The internal `Animated.View` adds a layer to the view hierarchy, which can cause unexpected layout behaviour with absolutely positioned or overlapping children.

pressable.subtitle: A primitive wrapper for building custom interactive elements with full press-lifecycle control.
pressable.description:
`Pressable` is the recommended primitive for building custom interactive elements. It wraps any children and exposes the full press interaction lifecycle — `onPressIn`, `onPress`, `onPressOut`, and `onLongPress` — without imposing any visual style of its own.

Both `style` and `children` can be functions that receive `{ pressed }`, making it easy to apply visual feedback directly in JSX without external state. This is the preferred way to dim, scale, or recolour an element during a press.

Use `hitSlop` to expand the touchable area beyond the visible bounds — especially useful for small icons and tight layouts — and `android_ripple` to configure the native ripple effect on Android.

props.onPress: Called when the press completes (after the finger lifts).
props.onPressIn: Called immediately when the touch engages — useful for instant feedback before `onPress` fires.
props.onLongPress: Called when a press lasts longer than 500ms; customise the threshold with `delayLongPress`.
props.hitSlop: Expands the touch-detection area beyond the component's visual bounds; accepts a number or `{ top, bottom, left, right }`.
props.disabled: When true, disables all press interactions.
props.android_ripple: Android only — configures the native ripple effect with `color`, `borderless`, and `radius`.

gotchas.gotcha1: *style and children can be functions* — Both accept `({ pressed }) => ...`, letting you apply press-state feedback directly in JSX without managing external state.
gotchas.gotcha2: *hitSlop vs pressRetentionOffset* — `hitSlop` defines where a press can start; `pressRetentionOffset` defines how far the finger can move after engaging while staying active. They are distinct concepts.
gotchas.gotcha3: *Touch area cannot exceed parent bounds* — `hitSlop` cannot expand the active area past the parent view; restructure the layout if a larger area is needed near an edge.
gotchas.gotcha4: *android_ripple foreground* — If child views have backgrounds or images that cover the ripple, add `foreground: true` to `android_ripple` so the effect renders on top.

switch.subtitle: A controlled boolean toggle for on/off settings.
switch.description:
`Switch` is a controlled boolean toggle — the mobile equivalent of a checkbox. Like all controlled inputs in React Native, it does not manage its own state: you must update the `value` prop in the `onValueChange` callback, or the switch will snap back after every tap.

Track and thumb colours can be customised independently. On iOS, `trackColor.false` only tints the track border when the switch is off — use `ios_backgroundColor` to control the full background colour in that state.

For accessibility, pair `Switch` with an adjacent `Text` label or use the `accessibilityLabel` prop so screen readers can announce what the toggle controls.

props.value: Current state of the switch; `true` means on.
props.onValueChange: Called with the new boolean when the user toggles the switch; must update `value` or the switch won't respond.
props.trackColor: Custom track colours for each state: `{ false: color, true: color }`.
props.thumbColor: Color of the thumb grip; on iOS, setting this removes the native drop shadow.
props.ios_backgroundColor: iOS only — background color visible when the switch is off or disabled; `trackColor` does not fill this area on iOS.

gotchas.gotcha1: *Must be fully controlled* — If `value` is not updated in `onValueChange`, the toggle snaps back after every tap. Always pair it with a state setter.
gotchas.gotcha2: *thumbColor removes the iOS shadow* — Setting `thumbColor` on iOS removes the native drop shadow from the grip, making it look different from the system default.
gotchas.gotcha3: *ios_backgroundColor vs trackColor on iOS* — `trackColor.false` only tints the track border when the switch is off; use `ios_backgroundColor` for the full background. This prop has no effect on Android.

activityindicator.subtitle: A circular loading spinner that communicates background activity to the user.
activityindicator.description:
`ActivityIndicator` renders the platform's native loading spinner. It is the standard way to communicate that the app is working — fetching data, processing a request, or waiting for a response.

The component is fully controlled: show and hide it by toggling the `animating` prop rather than conditionally mounting and unmounting it. On iOS, `hidesWhenStopped` (default `true`) automatically hides the spinner when `animating` is `false`; on Android you must manage visibility yourself.

Keep spinners contextual — place them close to the content that is loading rather than full-screen unless the entire UI is blocked.

props.animating: Whether to show the indicator (`true`) or hide it (`false`); defaults to `true`.
props.color: Foreground color of the spinner; defaults to the system accent on iOS and `#999999` on Android.
props.size: Size of the indicator: `'small'` or `'large'`; Android also accepts a number for a custom size.
props.hidesWhenStopped: iOS only — automatically hides the spinner when `animating` is `false`; has no effect on Android.

gotchas.gotcha1: *Default color differs by platform* — iOS uses the system accent color; Android defaults to `#999999`. Pass an explicit `color` for consistent branding across both platforms.
gotchas.gotcha2: *hidesWhenStopped is iOS only* — Android ignores this prop entirely; control visibility by toggling `animating` instead of relying on auto-hide.
gotchas.gotcha3: *Custom numeric size is Android only* — Passing a number to `size` only works on Android; on iOS only `'small'` and `'large'` are respected.

image.subtitle: Displays images from network URLs, local assets, and base64 data URIs.
image.description:
`Image` displays pictures from network URLs, local assets (via `require()`), and base64 data URIs. Its API is deliberately lean — dimensions and resize behaviour are controlled through the `style` prop and `resizeMode` rather than HTML-style attributes.

Network and data URI images have no inherent size, so React Native cannot infer their layout dimensions. You must explicitly set `width` and `height` in the style; omitting them causes the image to collapse to zero size and become invisible.

Use `onLoad` to react when the image finishes loading and `onError` to handle failures and show a fallback. For a smooth loading experience, provide a `defaultSource` (a local asset) to display while the real image fetches.

props.source: Specifies the image: a `{ uri }` object for network or data URIs, or `require()` for local assets.
props.resizeMode: Controls how the image scales when its frame doesn't match the raw dimensions (`cover`, `contain`, `stretch`, `repeat`, `center`).
props.style: Sets dimensions, border radius, and other visual properties; `width` and `height` are required for network images.
props.onLoad: Fires when the image finishes loading successfully; receives the actual source dimensions.
props.onError: Fires when the image fails to load; use it to set a flag and render a fallback.
props.defaultSource: Placeholder image shown while the actual source loads; ignored on Android debug builds.

gotchas.gotcha1: *Network images require explicit dimensions* — Unlike web ``, remote URLs carry no layout info; omitting `width` and `height` in the style collapses the image to zero size.
gotchas.gotcha2: *GIF and WebP on Android* — These formats are unsupported by default; add the Fresco animated modules to `android/app/build.gradle` to enable them.
gotchas.gotcha3: *defaultSource ignored on Android debug builds* — The placeholder only renders in release builds on Android; test placeholder behaviour on a release variant.
gotchas.gotcha4: *blurRadius differs by platform* — iOS needs values above 5 to produce a visible blur; the same value renders very differently between platforms.

textinput.subtitle: A text field that captures keyboard input with support for autocorrect, capitalization, and keyboard types.
textinput.description:
`TextInput` is the foundational component for capturing text in React Native. It is used in controlled mode by passing `value` and `onChangeText`, which receives the updated string on every keystroke.

Keyboard behaviour can be tuned with `keyboardType`, `autoCapitalize`, and `autoCorrect`. For passwords, use `secureTextEntry`, which masks characters with dots — but note that this prop is incompatible with `multiline`.

For programmatic access — moving focus or clearing the field — attach a `ref` and call methods like `focus()`, `blur()`, or `clear()`.

props.value: Controlled value; forces the native field to display this string.
props.onChangeText: Receives the updated string on every keystroke — the primary way to read input in a controlled component.
props.placeholder: Text shown before the user types anything.
props.keyboardType: Determines which keyboard layout opens (`default`, `email-address`, `numeric`, `phone-pad`, etc.).
props.secureTextEntry: Masks characters for passwords; incompatible with `multiline`.
props.multiline: Allows the field to grow across multiple lines.
props.editable: When false, the field is read-only and cannot be focused.
props.maxLength: Hard-limits the number of characters; prefer this over JS validation to avoid flicker.

gotchas.gotcha1: *Multiline vertical alignment* — On iOS text centers vertically in a multiline field; add `textAlignVertical: 'top'` to the style prop for cross-platform consistency.
gotchas.gotcha2: *Android underline* — Android renders a default bottom border on every TextInput; set `underlineColorAndroid="transparent"` to remove it.
gotchas.gotcha3: *secureTextEntry + multiline* — These two props cannot be used together; `secureTextEntry` is silently ignored when `multiline` is true.
gotchas.gotcha4: *Keyboard pushes layout on Android* — The soft keyboard may change `windowSoftInputMode`, breaking absolutely positioned components. Wrap the screen in `KeyboardAvoidingView` to manage this.

scrollview.subtitle: A scrollable container that renders all its children at once.
scrollview.description:
`ScrollView` wraps the platform's native scroll view and integrates with React Native's touch responder system. Unlike `FlatList`, it renders all children immediately — so it is best suited for content of bounded, predictable size.

The scroll container must have a bounded height to work correctly. On a vertical `ScrollView` this means at least one ancestor must have an explicit height or `flex: 1` propagated down the tree. The most common layout bug is a `ScrollView` with unbounded height that never scrolls.

For programmatic control, attach a `ref` and call `scrollTo` or `scrollToEnd`. On iOS, `scrollEventThrottle` controls how often `onScroll` fires — set it to `16` to get approximately one event per frame.

props.horizontal: Arranges children in a horizontal row instead of a vertical column.
props.contentContainerStyle: Styles applied to the inner content container that wraps all children.
props.showsVerticalScrollIndicator: Shows or hides the vertical scroll indicator; also `showsHorizontalScrollIndicator` for horizontal mode.
props.pagingEnabled: Snaps scrolling to multiples of the scroll view's size, enabling swipeable page-by-page navigation.
props.onScroll: Fired during scrolling with position data; pair with `scrollEventThrottle` to control frequency on iOS.
props.scrollEnabled: When false, content cannot be scrolled via touch interaction but can still scroll programmatically.

gotchas.gotcha1: *Height must be bounded* — ScrollView needs a constrained height to scroll. A missing `flex: 1` on an ancestor is the most common cause of a scroll view that never scrolls.
gotchas.gotcha2: *Not for long lists* — All children render immediately. For datasets with many items use `FlatList` or `SectionList`, which render lazily.
gotchas.gotcha3: *Nested scrolling on Android* — Mixing horizontal and vertical ScrollViews requires `nestedScrollEnabled={true}` on Android; without it, inner scrolling is blocked.
gotchas.gotcha4: *scrollEventThrottle on iOS* — `onScroll` fires at most once per frame by default on iOS; set `scrollEventThrottle={16}` for per-frame updates when tracking scroll position.

flatlist.subtitle: A performant scrollable list for rendering flat data arrays.
flatlist.description:
`FlatList` is a high-performance list component built on top of `ScrollView`. It renders items lazily — only the ones currently on screen (plus a configurable window) — making it suitable for long or unknown-length datasets.

Because `FlatList` extends `PureComponent`, it uses shallow equality to decide whether to re-render. If your `renderItem` depends on state or context outside `data`, you must pass that value to `extraData` so the list knows to update.

For best scrolling performance, provide `getItemLayout` when items have a fixed height. This lets `FlatList` skip dynamic measurement and jump directly to any position in the list.

props.data: The array of items to render. Each element is passed to `renderItem`.
props.renderItem: Receives `{item, index, separators}` and must return a React element.
props.keyExtractor: Returns a unique string key for each item; falls back to `item.key`, then `item.id`, then the index.
props.extraData: Any value that, when changed, forces the list to re-render — needed for state-dependent rows.
props.getItemLayout: Returns `{length, offset, index}` for fixed-size items, enabling scroll-to and skipping dynamic measurement.
props.numColumns: Renders items in a grid with this many columns; items must be the same height.

gotchas.gotcha1: *extraData required for state-driven rows* — FlatList is a PureComponent; without extraData, selecting or toggling an item won't update its render.
gotchas.gotcha2: *Internal state is not preserved* — When a row scrolls off screen it unmounts. Manage selection and other state outside the list.
gotchas.gotcha3: *Blank rows while fast-scrolling* — FlatList renders asynchronously; fast scrolls can reveal empty areas. Use `getItemLayout` and tune `windowSize` to mitigate.
gotchas.gotcha4: *numColumns requires uniform height* — Multi-column layouts don't support items of different heights; use a masonry library instead.

button.subtitle: A basic pressable element that triggers an action. Renders as a native button on each platform.
button.description:
A basic pressable element that triggers an action. Renders as a native button on each platform — a `UIButton` on iOS and an `android.widget.Button` on Android — so its appearance follows the OS styling by default.

Because `Button` delegates rendering to the platform, its customisation surface is intentionally minimal. For full visual control use `Pressable` or `TouchableOpacity` instead.

The `onPress` handler receives a `GestureResponderEvent` but it is rarely needed; most handlers ignore the argument entirely.

props.title: Text label rendered inside the button. On Android the OS automatically uppercases it.
props.onPress: Fired when the button is pressed.
props.color: Text color on iOS, background color on Android.
props.disabled: Disables interaction and renders in a muted style.

gotchas.gotcha1: *iOS vs Android* — `color` sets the text color on iOS but the background fill on Android. A single value will look inconsistent across platforms.
gotchas.gotcha2: *No style prop* — `Button` doesn't accept `style`. Any customisation beyond `color` and `disabled` requires `Pressable` or `TouchableOpacity`.
gotchas.gotcha3: *Android* — The `title` is automatically uppercased by the OS. Use `Pressable` with a `Text` child if case matters.
gotchas.gotcha4: *Touch target* — React Native recommends 44×44pt minimum. `Button` doesn't enforce this — small labels produce small buttons.
